questions.question: The instrument's pulser hits the element with a several-hundred-volt spike and the element physically changes thickness, launching a wave into the part. The element is using:
questions.explanation: Transmitting is the inverse effect — applied voltage produces mechanical deformation. The direct effect (pressure producing voltage) is the receiving mechanism.

questions.question: A probe maker needs a 2.25 MHz element from PZT-type ceramic (v ≈ 4,000 m/s = 4.0 mm/µs). The element must be ground to a thickness of about:
questions.explanation: t = v ÷ (2f) = 4.0 ÷ (2 × 2.25) ≈ 0.89 mm — half a wavelength in the element material at the resonant frequency.

questions.question: A probe specification calls for an element material whose own internal structure damps the ringing, giving naturally short, broadband pulses for high-resolution work. That material is:
questions.explanation: Lead metaniobate is the internally damped specialist: short clean pulses and excellent resolution at some cost in sensitivity. Quartz is the inefficient historic transmitter; lithium sulfate was the classic receiver.

questions.question: A 6 mm diameter, 5 MHz probe transmits longitudinal waves into steel (λ = 1.18 mm). Its near-field length is approximately:
questions.explanation: N = D² ÷ (4λ) = 36 ÷ 4.72 ≈ 7.6 mm. The 21 mm value belongs to a 10 mm element at the same frequency — diameter is squared in the formula.

questions.question: Keeping frequency the same, an inspector swaps a 10 mm element for a 20 mm element. The effect on the beam is:
options: Near field four times longer and a narrower spread angle
Near field four times shorter and a wider spread angle
Near field twice as long with unchanged spread
No change — beam shape depends only on frequency
questions.explanation: N = D²/4λ, so doubling D quadruples the near field; sin γ = 1.22λ/D, so doubling D halves the sine of the half-angle — a longer near field and a tighter beam.

questions.question: Along the beam axis, the strongest (and final) intensity maximum occurs:
options: At the near-field distance N, where the far field begins
questions.explanation: Interference inside the near field creates fluctuating maxima and minima; the last and largest axial maximum sits at N, beyond which amplitude decays smoothly with distance.

questions.question: For precision thickness readings on thin sections, a probe with a replaceable plastic standoff between the element and the part is chosen because it:
options: Moves the initial pulse and much of the near field out of the test metal
Converts the longitudinal wave to a shear wave
Eliminates the need for couplant between standoff and part
questions.explanation: That is the delay-line probe: the plastic delay carries the initial pulse and near-field turbulence, so the thin metal section is measured by the clean far-field beam, with couplant still needed at the contact.

questions.question: A mill needs to inspect red-hot steel sections from a standoff distance with no physical contact and no couplant of any kind. The technology suited to this is:
options: A dual-element probe with high-temperature delay lines
An immersion tank with focused probes
Laser ultrasonics — laser generation with interferometric detection
questions.explanation: Laser UT generates sound thermoelastically with one laser and reads surface vibration with another, fully non-contact at standoff — practical on glowing-hot or untouchable parts. EMATs are couplant-free but must sit close to the surface; immersion and delay lines still involve contact media.

questions.question: Switching couplant from water (Z ≈ 1.5 MRayl) to glycerin (Z ≈ 2.4 MRayl) on the same steel part chiefly buys you:
options: More energy transmitted into the steel — roughly 19% versus 12% per pass
questions.explanation: Glycerin's higher impedance reduces the mismatch with steel, raising one-way energy transmission from about 12% to about 19% — roughly half again better, which matters when sensitivity is marginal. It actually increases cleanup work.

questions.question: A procedure for examining austenitic stainless steel piping in a nuclear plant requires certification paperwork for the couplant. The certification most likely limits:
options: Sulfur and halogen (chloride) content, which can promote corrosion and stress-corrosion cracking
The couplant's color, for visibility on the part
Acoustic impedance, which must exactly match the pipe
Viscosity, which must be below that of water
questions.explanation: On austenitic stainless, nickel alloys, and titanium, sulfur and halogens are controlled because residues can drive corrosion and SCC. Low-S/low-halogen certified couplants — with certs on file — are standard nuclear and aerospace practice.
